description: When proposers disagree on architecture choice, the synthesized response should surface the trade-off, not silently pick one side (PM #39).

input.message: For a small team building a real-time multiplayer game backend, should I use Go with goroutines or Node.js with Socket.io? Give me the recommendation.

mock_response:
Both stacks can serve this workload — the choice depends on your team's comfort and the specific scaling shape.

**Go + goroutines** is the right pick if: (a) you expect >5k concurrent connections per node, (b) you have someone on the team comfortable with Go's concurrency primitives, (c) low memory-per-connection matters. Goroutines scale to hundreds of thousands of connections on modest hardware.

**Node.js + Socket.io** is the right pick if: (a) the team is already JavaScript-fluent, (b) you want to share types/code between client and server, (c) the connection count is moderate (<5k per node), (d) you value the ecosystem (Socket.io's room/namespace abstractions are excellent for game lobbies).

There is a real trade-off here. If forced to pick blind, I'd lean Node.js for a small team starting out — the iteration speed advantage usually outweighs Go's runtime efficiency at the early-stage scale. Revisit if you cross the 5k connection threshold.
